findings.description: reachable() enqueues every neighbor with no 'not in seen' guard, so on the cyclic graph its own docstring promises to support (e.g. A->B, B->A) the queue grows without bound and the loop never terminates.
count_paths() recurses with no cycle detection or recursion guard despite only 'DAG assumed'; any cyclic input causes infinite recursion / RecursionError, and very deep DAGs can also blow the stack.

Even on acyclic input the BFS queue is never deduplicated, so shared descendants in a diamond/lattice graph are re-enqueued and re-expanded once per incoming edge, degrading to exponential traversal work.

Pure query methods index self._edges[node] (a defaultdict), which inserts an empty list for leaf/unknown nodes as a read side effect, silently growing internal state and violating the query-does-not-mutate invariant (also lines 40, 54, 61).
reachable() never adds `start` to `seen`, so the start node is reported as not reachable from itself; this self-reachability semantics is undocumented and likely surprising to callers.
reachable(), count_paths(), and neighbors() silently return empty/0/[] for a node absent from _nodes instead of signaling an error, masking caller bugs that pass invalid node ids (also lines 35, 60).
topological_order() returns None to signal a cycle but this overloads None and is undocumented, so callers cannot distinguish 'graph has a cycle' from any other empty/failure result.
`Iterable` is imported but never used anywhere in the module.
